comment_text.sn11.4:1.3: Vepacitti (“wise thinker”) was the lord of the asuras and Sakka’s counterpart; their relationship status is complicated. The Sanskrit form is vipracitti, but in Buddhist texts it is incorrectly Sanskritized to vemacitra. He seems not to appear in early Sanskrit texts, but the Mahābhārata and Purāṇas regard him as the eldest son of Danū and hence chief of the Dānavas.
comment_text.sn11.4:1.9: It seems they had strong feelings for each other.
comment_text.sn11.4:1.10: Mātali is the charioteer of Sakka. He appears at DN 20:10.8 as one of the centaur lords, and is father to the centaur Sikhaṇḍī, Pañcasikha’s rival in love (DN 21:1.6.8). In Pali, he is never addressed with the usual word for charioteer (sārathi), but by the epithet saṅgāhaka, literally “gatherer” in the sense “one who holds the reins”, here “chariot handler”, recalling the centaur who took the reins from Indra (Rig Veda 1.163.2). Later Brahmanical literature also knows him as Indra’s charioteer, but pre-Buddhist references are few and obscure. He flourishes in the land of Yama along with the poets (Rig Veda 10.14.3 = Atharva Veda 18.1.47); he is made of magic (Atharva Veda 8.9.5); he knows the immortal balm bought by a chariot’s price that is infused by Indra in the waters (Atharva Veda 11.6.23).
comment_text.sn11.4:2.1: For Sakka’s epithets, see SN 11.12 and notes there.
comment_text.sn11.4:4.4: Throughout this chapter, Mātali displays more of the old martial character, and Sakka leads him to growth, as he does for the titans.
comment_text.sn11.4:7.3: I take sadatthaparamā atthā as truncated dative of purpose; see discussion at SN 11.8:2.2.

root_text.sn11.4:1.3: Atha kho, bhikkhave, vepacitti asurindo asure āmantesi:
root_text.sn11.4:1.4: ‘sace, mārisā, devānaṁ asurasaṅgāme samupabyūḷhe asurā jineyyuṁ devā parājineyyuṁ, yena naṁ sakkaṁ devānamindaṁ kaṇṭhapañcamehi bandhanehi bandhitvā mama santike āneyyātha asurapuran’ti.
root_text.sn11.4:1.5: Sakkopi kho, bhikkhave, devānamindo deve tāvatiṁse āmantesi:
root_text.sn11.4:1.6: ‘sace, mārisā, devānaṁ asurasaṅgāme samupabyūḷhe devā jineyyuṁ asurā parājineyyuṁ, yena naṁ vepacittiṁ asurindaṁ kaṇṭhapañcamehi bandhanehi bandhitvā mama santike āneyyātha sudhammasabhan’ti.
root_text.sn11.4:1.7: Tasmiṁ kho pana, bhikkhave, saṅgāme devā jiniṁsu, asurā parājiniṁsu.
root_text.sn11.4:1.8: Atha kho, bhikkhave, devā tāvatiṁsā vepacittiṁ asurindaṁ kaṇṭhapañcamehi bandhanehi bandhitvā sakkassa devānamindassa santike ānesuṁ sudhammasabhaṁ.
root_text.sn11.4:1.9: Tatra sudaṁ, bhikkhave, vepacitti asurindo kaṇṭhapañcamehi bandhanehi baddho sakkaṁ devānamindaṁ sudhammasabhaṁ pavisantañca nikkhamantañca asabbhāhi pharusāhi vācāhi akkosati paribhāsati.
root_text.sn11.4:1.10: Atha kho, bhikkhave, mātali saṅgāhako sakkaṁ devānamindaṁ gāthāhi ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn11.4:13.1: So hi nāma, bhikkhave, sakko devānamindo sakaṁ puññaphalaṁ upajīvamāno devānaṁ tāvatiṁsānaṁ issariyādhipaccaṁ rajjaṁ kārento khantisoraccassa vaṇṇavādī bhavissati.
root_text.sn11.4:13.2: Idha kho taṁ, bhikkhave, sobhetha yaṁ tumhe evaṁ svākkhāte dhammavinaye pabbajitā samānā khamā ca bhaveyyātha soratā cā”ti.

translation_text.sn11.4:1.2: “Once upon a time, mendicants, a battle was fought between the gods and the titans.
translation_text.sn11.4:1.3: Then Vepacitti, lord of titans, addressed the titans,
translation_text.sn11.4:1.4: ‘My good fellows, if the titans defeat the gods in this battle, bind Sakka, the lord of gods, by his limbs and neck and bring him to my presence in the citadel of the titans.’
translation_text.sn11.4:1.5: Meanwhile, Sakka, lord of gods, addressed the gods of the thirty-three,
translation_text.sn11.4:1.6: ‘My good fellows, if the gods defeat the titans in this battle, bind Vepacitti by his limbs and neck and bring him to my presence in the Hall of Clear Right of the gods.’
translation_text.sn11.4:1.7: In that battle the gods won and the titans lost.
translation_text.sn11.4:1.8: So the gods of the thirty-three bound Vepacitti by his limbs and neck and brought him to Sakka’s presence in the Hall of Clear Right of the gods.
translation_text.sn11.4:1.9: And as Sakka was entering and leaving the hall, Vepacitti abused and insulted him with rude, harsh words.
translation_text.sn11.4:1.10: So Mātali the chariot handler addressed Sakka in verse,
translation_text.sn11.4:2.2: is it from fear or from weakness
translation_text.sn11.4:2.3: that you withstand such harsh words
translation_text.sn11.4:3.1: ‘It’s not out of fear or weakness
translation_text.sn11.4:3.3: For how can a sensible person like me
translation_text.sn11.4:3.4: get in a fight with a fool?’
translation_text.sn11.4:4.2: if there’s no-one to put a stop to them.
translation_text.sn11.4:4.3: So a wise one should stop
translation_text.sn11.4:5.1: ‘I think that this is the only way
translation_text.sn11.4:5.2: to put a stop to a fool,
translation_text.sn11.4:5.3: when you know that the other is upset,
translation_text.sn11.4:6.5: the simpleton will go after you even harder,
translation_text.sn11.4:6.6: like a cow chasing someone who runs away.’
translation_text.sn11.4:7.1: ‘Let him think this if he wishes, or not—
translation_text.sn11.4:7.3: For the highest goal, one’s own,
translation_text.sn11.4:7.4: naught better than patience is found.
translation_text.sn11.4:8.3: they call that the ultimate patience,
translation_text.sn11.4:8.4: for a weakling must always be patient.
translation_text.sn11.4:9.2: is really just weakness, they say.
translation_text.sn11.4:9.3: But no-one can challenge a person
translation_text.sn11.4:9.4: who’s strong, guarded by the teaching.
translation_text.sn11.4:10.1: When you get angry at an angry person
translation_text.sn11.4:10.2: you just make things worse for yourself.
translation_text.sn11.4:10.3: When you don’t get angry at an angry person
translation_text.sn11.4:10.4: you win a battle hard to win.
translation_text.sn11.4:11.1: When you know that the other is angry,
translation_text.sn11.4:11.2: you act for the good of both
translation_text.sn11.4:11.4: if you’re mindful and stay calm.
translation_text.sn11.4:13.1: Since, mendicants, even that Sakka, lord of gods—while living off of the fruit of his good deeds, and ruling as sovereign lord over these gods of the thirty-three—will speak in praise of patience and gentleness,
translation_text.sn11.4:13.2: you can excel at this, for you who have gone forth in such a well explained teaching and training can develop patience and gentleness.”
